readAloud.expectedAnswerFormat: Full text read aloud with natural pronunciation and fluency. No fixed expected answer; evaluated on content delivery, pronunciation, and fluency.

repeatSentence.expectedAnswerFormat: Exact repetition of the heard sentence. Evaluated on accuracy, pronunciation, and fluency.

describeImage.expectedAnswerFormat: Detailed description of the image covering key features, trends, and relationships. Evaluated on content, pronunciation, fluency, and structure.

retellLecture.expectedAnswerFormat: Summary of the lecture in own words covering main points. Evaluated on content, pronunciation, fluency, and structure.

answerShortQuestion.expectedAnswerFormat: Short, direct answer (1-3 words). See individual question answers.

summarizeWrittenText.expectedAnswerFormat: Single sentence summarizing the key points of the passage. See sample answers for each passage.

writeEssay.expectedAnswerFormat: Well-organized essay with clear position, supporting arguments, and examples. Evaluated on content, organization, grammar, vocabulary, and cohesion.

summarizeSpokenText.expectedAnswerFormat: Concise summary (50-70 words) covering the main points of the lecture. Evaluated on content, grammar, vocabulary, and conciseness.

tasks.prompt: Look at the text below. In 40 seconds, you must read this text aloud as naturally and as clearly as possible. You have 40 seconds to read aloud.
tasks.expectedAnswerFormat: Read the full text naturally and clearly.

items.title: Italian alphabet
items.text: The Italian alphabet has fewer letters in comparison with the English alphabet. Italian does not use the letters J, K, W, X or Y – except in borrowed words. However, young Italians are increasingly using the letter K in words that would be written with C or CH in standard Italian orthography.
items.expectedAnswer: Read aloud the provided text with clear pronunciation, appropriate pace, and natural intonation. No exact answer; evaluated on delivery.

items.title: Summerhill School
items.text: Summerhill School was regarded with considerable suspicion by the educational establishment. Lessons were optional for pupils at the school, and the government of the school was carried out by a School Council, of which all the pupils and staff were members, with everyone having equal voting rights.
items.expectedAnswer: Read aloud the provided text with clear pronunciation, appropriate pace, and natural intonation. No exact answer; evaluated on delivery.

items.title: Interview workshops
items.text: This term the University is running a series of workshops for final year students on how to do well in interviews. These sessions will help participants prepare effectively for – and perform at their best during – later job interviews. The workshop tutors have an excellent record of success in helping students acquire the positions they desire.
items.expectedAnswer: Read aloud the provided text with clear pronunciation, appropriate pace, and natural intonation. No exact answer; evaluated on delivery.

items.title: Tasmania
items.text: Tasmania is a large and relatively sparsely populated island off the south coast of Australia. The island is of particular interest to natural scientists, who go there to research the unique wildlife. Tasmania has, for example, twelve species of bird that are not found anywhere else in the world.
items.expectedAnswer: Read aloud the provided text with clear pronunciation, appropriate pace, and natural intonation. No exact answer; evaluated on delivery.

items.title: Honey
items.text: Honey has traditionally been credited with significant medical powers, and it has played a major part in many folk remedies. But it seems now its efficacy is not just an old wives' tale. Recent research has shown there is scientific evidence to prove that honey contains elements that prevent bacteria from growing.
items.expectedAnswer: Read aloud the provided text with clear pronunciation, appropriate pace, and natural intonation. No exact answer; evaluated on delivery.

items.title: College museum
items.text: The College has a fascinating museum dedicated to archaeology and anthropology. It contains information about many of the studies which have been carried out by members of the College over the five hundred years of its existence. There are many unique exhibits brought back from excavations and explorations in all the continents.
items.expectedAnswer: Read aloud the provided text with clear pronunciation, appropriate pace, and natural intonation. No exact answer; evaluated on delivery.

tasks.prompt: Look at the [map/graph/diagram/table/chart/chart] below. In 25 seconds, please speak into the microphone and describe in detail what the [map/graph/diagram/table/chart/chart] is showing. You will have 40 seconds to give your response.
tasks.expectedAnswerFormat: Detailed description of the image: type of graph, axes/labels, key trends, comparisons, and conclusions.

items.title: Antarctica
items.description: Map showing the continent of Antarctica: roughly circular shape, diameter ~4,000 km, three oceans (Atlantic, Indian, Pacific), South Pole near centre, McMurdo Station on Ross Sea, Ross Ice Shelf.
items.expectedAnswer: This is a map of Antarctica, the southernmost continent. It has a roughly circular shape with a diameter of approximately 4,000 kilometers. The continent is surrounded by three oceans: the Atlantic, Indian, and Pacific. The South Pole is located near the center, with McMurdo Station situated on the Ross Sea and the Ross Ice Shelf visible on the eastern side.

items.title: Temperatures in the mountains of Southwest Tasmania
items.description: Graph showing average maximum and minimum daily temperatures for each month, plus recorded highest and lowest temperatures; coldest months June/July, hottest January/February; average summer temperatures 2-12°C, winter -5-2°C.
items.expectedAnswer: This graph displays temperature data for the mountains of Southwest Tasmania. It shows average maximum and minimum daily temperatures for each month, along with recorded highest and lowest extremes. The coldest months are June and July, with average temperatures ranging from -5 to 2°C. The hottest months are January and February, with summer averages between 2 and 12°C.

items.title: Life cycle of a plant
items.description: Diagram of a sunflower life cycle: digging soil → sowing seed → roots grow, stem appears → stem grows taller (rain and sun) → leaves sprout → flower blooms → seeds in centre → cycle repeats.
items.expectedAnswer: This diagram illustrates the life cycle of a sunflower plant. The cycle begins with digging the soil and sowing a seed. Roots grow and a stem appears, which grows taller with rain and sunlight. Leaves sprout, followed by a blooming flower. Seeds form in the center of the flower, and the cycle repeats when the seeds are dispersed and germinate.

items.title: Export of motorcycles from Japan
items.description: Table showing exports by region with percentages and percentage change from previous year: Asia (7.7%, -4.7%), Middle East (0.7%, -2.9%), Europe (34.5%, -23.6%), North America (35.5%, +68.7%), Central America (1.2%, +11.6%), South America (8.0%, +19.9%), Africa (4.2%, -17.8%), Oceania (8.2%, -16.7%), Total (100.0%, +2.3%).
items.expectedAnswer: This table shows Japanese motorcycle exports by region. Europe and North America are the largest markets at 34.5% and 35.5% respectively. North America showed the strongest growth at +68.7%, while Europe declined by -23.6%. South America and Central America also showed positive growth, while Asia, Africa, and Oceania all declined. Overall exports grew by 2.3%.

items.title: Employment of Physics graduates one year after graduation
items.description: Pie chart showing: Full-time education (35%), Part-time study with work (15%), Full-time paid work (30%), Part-time work (5%), Unpaid work (5%), Unemployed (7%), Not available for work (3%).
items.expectedAnswer: This pie chart shows the employment status of Physics graduates one year after graduation. 35% are in full-time education, 15% are in part-time study with work, and 30% are in full-time paid work. Part-time work and unpaid work each account for 5%, unemployment is 7%, and 3% are not available for work. Overall, 65% are engaged in some form of work.

items.title: Contribution of selected sectors to the UK economy in the twentieth century
items.description: Bar chart showing three sectors (Agriculture, Manufacturing, Business & Finance) from 1900-2000: Agriculture peaked ~53% in 1950 then fell to ~2% by 2000; Manufacturing declined steadily; Business & Finance grew rapidly to over 33% by 2000.
items.expectedAnswer: This bar chart shows the contribution of three sectors to the UK economy over the twentieth century. Agriculture peaked at about 53% in 1950 but declined sharply to approximately 2% by 2000. Manufacturing showed a steady decline throughout the century. Business and Finance grew rapidly, particularly after 1950, reaching over 33% by 2000, reflecting the shift from a manufacturing to a service-based economy.

tasks.prompt: You will hear a lecture. After listening to the lecture, in 10 seconds, please speak into the microphone and retell what you have just heard from the lecture in your own words. You will have 40 seconds to give your response.
tasks.expectedAnswerFormat: Oral summary of the lecture in own words covering main ideas and key details.

items.title: History of salt
items.audioScriptSummary: Salt was a highly valued commodity in ancient times for preserving food; allowed transport of food long distances; Romans built roads to transport salt; raised salt price to fund wars; African caravans of up to 40,000 camels transported salt across Sahara to Timbuktu.
items.expectedAnswer: The lecture discusses the historical importance of salt as a valuable commodity. Salt was essential for preserving food, enabling long-distance food transport. The Romans built roads specifically for salt transportation and raised salt prices to fund military campaigns. In Africa, caravans of up to 40,000 camels transported salt across the Sahara Desert to Timbuktu.

items.title: Children's literature
items.audioScriptSummary: Children's literature has educational purpose to teach moral values; uses heroes children admire; heroes demonstrate courage, consideration, loyalty, respect for work; examples from Aesop (ant vs. grasshopper) and Cinderella show hard work rewarded; stories remain entertaining despite being instructive.
items.expectedAnswer: The lecture explores the educational purpose of children's literature. Stories are designed to teach moral values through heroes that children admire. These heroes demonstrate virtues such as courage, consideration, loyalty, and respect for work. Examples like Aesop's fable of the ant and grasshopper and Cinderella show that hard work is rewarded. Despite their instructive nature, the stories remain entertaining.

items.title: Electric vehicles
items.audioScriptSummary: Electric cars existed at beginning of 20th century and were more comfortable than combustion engines; petrol cars dominated; electric used for specific purposes (fork-lifts, ambulances, delivery vehicles); Switzerland used electric trains due to lack of coal/oil; renewed interest today due to oil costs and environmental concerns.
items.expectedAnswer: The lecture traces the history of electric vehicles. Electric cars existed at the beginning of the 20th century and were more comfortable than combustion engine vehicles. However, petrol cars eventually dominated the market. Electric vehicles were used for specific purposes like fork-lifts, ambulances, and delivery vehicles. Switzerland notably used electric trains due to coal and oil shortages. There is renewed interest today because of rising oil costs and environmental concerns.

items.question: At what ceremony do students receive their degree or diploma at the end of their period of study?

tasks.prompt: Read the passage below and summarize it using one sentence. Type your response in the box at the bottom of the screen. You have 10 minutes to finish this task. Your response will be judged on the quality of your writing and on how well your response presents the key points in the passage.
tasks.expectedAnswerFormat: One sentence summarizing the key points of the passage.

items.title: Mindfulness
items.text: Passage about international companies encouraging staff to use meditation/mindfulness; belief that it positively impacts performance and workplace relationships.
items.expectedAnswer: International companies are increasingly encouraging employees to practice mindfulness and meditation, believing it enhances workplace performance, improves mental well-being, and fosters better relationships among colleagues.

items.title: Academic research
items.text: Passage about criticisms from politicians/business people that research is pointless; academics defend research with standard objections; author adds personal point about the power of chance, using their experience with a police officer student and the book that resulted.
items.expectedAnswer: Despite criticism from politicians and business leaders that research is pointless, academics defend it through standard arguments, but the author emphasizes the power of chance, illustrated by their encounter with a police officer student that led to a book.

items.title: Physical activity and educational performance
items.text: Passage about mixed/inconsistent research results on exercise and academic success; cross-sectional studies show positive correlation but direction of cause unclear; longitudinal studies support benefits but criticized for methodology; exercise has small beneficial cognitive impact.
items.expectedAnswer: Research on the link between physical activity and academic performance shows mixed results, with cross-sectional studies finding positive correlations but unclear causation, while longitudinal studies support benefits despite methodological criticisms, indicating only a small beneficial cognitive impact.

tasks.prompt: You will have 20 minutes to plan, write and revise an essay about the topic below. Your response will be judged on how well you develop a position, organize your ideas, present supporting details, and control the elements of standard written English. You should write 200-300 words.
tasks.expectedAnswerFormat: Well-structured essay with clear introduction, body paragraphs, and conclusion.

items.topic: 'Computer technology has had more of a negative than a positive impact on society.' How far do you agree with this statement? Support your views with reasons and/or examples from your own experience.
items.expectedAnswer: Sample essay structure: Introduction - present the debate and state position. Body paragraph 1 - positive impacts (connectivity, access to information, automation, healthcare advances, education). Body paragraph 2 - negative impacts (social isolation, privacy concerns, job displacement, screen addiction). Body paragraph 3 - personal experience/observations. Conclusion - balanced view acknowledging both benefits and drawbacks, conclude that overall impact has been positive despite challenges.

tasks.prompt: Read the text and answer the multiple-choice question by selecting the correct response. Only one response is correct.

items.title: Christ Church Cathedral clock
items.text: Passage about Christ Church Cathedral clock in Oxford being five minutes late; due to conservative clergy who wanted the idea to prove its worth; railways in 19th century made synchronisation important.
items.question: Why does Christ Church Cathedral clock give a different time from other British churches?
options: A) Oxford likes to be different from other places.
B) It was initially a protest against the coming of the railway.
C) Cathedral staff were reluctant to act too quickly.
D) It was very difficult to alter the time on the clock.
items.expectedAnswer: C

items.title: Zebras
items.text: Passage about zebras being members of horse family but not used for riding; neck too stiff, back structure unsuitable; possible to breed for riding but internal combustion engine removed incentive.
items.question: What is the writer's conclusion about zebras?
options: A) Zebras are not as similar to horses as they initially appear.
B) People have little motivation to develop a zebra that could be ridden.
C) It is unlikely that people could develop a zebra that would be good to ride.
D) Motorised vehicles are gradually reducing the numbers of zebras that are being bred.
items.expectedAnswer: B

items.expectedAnswer: C → B → E → D → A

tasks.prompt: You will hear a short lecture. Write a summary for a fellow student who was not present at the lecture. You should write 50-70 words.
tasks.expectedAnswerFormat: Concise summary (50-70 words) covering the main points.

items.title: Allergies
items.topic: Allergies are the body reacting to harmful substances; histamines produced to remove irritants; allergies more common in hygienic societies where immune system has less to fight; sufferers should avoid triggers or take anti-histamines.
items.expectedAnswer: Allergies occur when the body reacts to harmful substances, producing histamines to remove irritants. They are more common in hygienic societies where the immune system has fewer challenges. Sufferers should avoid triggers or use anti-histamines to manage symptoms.

items.title: European views of America
items.topic: Europeans have long been interested in reports about America, partly due to emigration; two views emerged: America is unique (inimitable) or America is the forerunner of future society.
items.expectedAnswer: Europeans have long been fascinated by reports about America, partly driven by emigration. Two contrasting views emerged: one sees America as unique and inimitable, while the other views it as a forerunner of future society, representing what other countries might become.

items.title: Translation tips
items.question: What tips does the speaker give about translation?
options: A) Always translate literally to maintain accuracy.
B) Ask a native speaker if you are not sure of the meaning of something.
C) Use online translation tools for technical texts.
D) Don't translate technical texts unless you are familiar with the subject.
E) Translate every word to ensure nothing is missed.

items.question: What qualities does the speaker say architects should consider?
options: A) how attractive the building is to look at
B) how many people will use the building
C) how much the building will cost to construct
D) how long the building is likely to last
E) how the building fits with surrounding architecture

tasks.prompt: You will hear a recording. Type the missing words in each blank.
You will hear a recording. Click on the paragraph that best relates to the recording.

items.title: The human hand

summaries.text: What makes people unique compared to other creatures is their hands. Their flexibility has allowed us to develop the manual skills that have made society what it is today. People should become more aware of the complicated anatomy of each of their hands.

The thumb is the part of our hand which is most complicated in terms of the number of muscles which are required to control it. However, each of our fingers also depends on a set of nerves and muscles which enables it to carry out an extraordinary variety of different actions.

The amazing flexibility of the human hand is truly remarkable. It is the result of a complex anatomy lying under the skin of the hand, and it can be useful to study this anatomy if you want to learn how to use your hands more effectively for sporting or other purposes.

The human hand is extraordinarily flexible. It enables a person to do a remarkable variety of things, some demanding great precision and others requiring considerable strength. This is because of the complex structure of nerves, muscles and ligaments that makes up a hand.

items.expectedAnswer: D

items.title: Great White sharks

summaries.text: There are fewer Great White sharks in Australian waters than was once believed. This is because tagging has shown that sharks travel considerable distances, and a shark recorded east of Bass Strait one week is often recorded west of Bass Strait the next. However, sharks always return to their place of origin to breed.

A recent research study has shown that Australian Great White shark populations have remained surprisingly distinct as, despite travelling long distances, these sharks do not breed away from their original areas. This means that local shark habitats may have a greater effect on sharks than has been believed up to now.

There is a greater variety in the Great White shark populations in Australian waters than was previously thought to be the case. This means that some types of shark are actually more endangered than was believed. Scientists are therefore developing conservation programs which will help to protect these threatened species.

An investigation of Great White sharks in Australian waters has come up with some unexpected conclusions, as it found that the genetic make-up of sharks in one area was quite distinct from those found elsewhere. This made scientists realize that sharks do not swim as far away from their home areas as used to be thought.

items.expectedAnswer: B

items.title: Spread of English
items.transcriptWithErrors: The English language has been spreading across the globe for centuries. It moved from its origins in England to other countries as explorers passed through new territories. The path of English expansion followed trade routes around the earth. Today, English is spoken in many places as a first or second language. The concepts in which English is used have expanded dramatically.

The findings are significant, and the researchers freely recommend moderate chocolate consumption as part of a healthy diet.

comparisonWithAllTests.notes: Practice Test 4 differs slightly from the other three tests: it has 3 Summarize Written Text tasks (instead of 2) and only 1 Essay topic (instead of 2). All other task types and quantities remain consistent across all four practice tests.
